questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $2 \text{ kg}$ is situated on a rough inclined plane of inclination $30^\circ$. Determine the magnitude of the force parallel to the incline needed to make the block move down the incline. The coefficient of static friction is $0.2$.
questions.solution:
The component of the weight of the block acting down the inclined plane is:

$W_{\parallel} = mg \sin 30^\circ$

$W_{\parallel} = 2 \times 9.8 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = 9.8 \text{ N}$

The maximum static friction force acting up the incline is:

$f_{\text{max}} = \mu_s N = \mu_s mg \cos 30^\circ$

$f_{\text{max}} = 0.2 \times 2 \times 9.8 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 3.39 \text{ N}$

Since the downward driving force ($9.8 \text{ N}$) is strictly greater than the maximum static friction ($3.39 \text{ N}$), the block will slide down the incline under its own weight.

Therefore, no additional external force is required to make the block move down the incline. The required force is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $4 \text{ kg}$ slides down an inclined surface having an inclination of $30^\circ$ with the horizontal. Starting from rest, it covers $8 \text{ m}$ during the first two seconds. Suppose the block is pushed down the incline by a force of $4 \text{ N}$. Determine how far the block will move in the first two seconds after starting from rest.
questions.solution:
Let the initial acceleration of the block be $a_1$.

Using the equation of motion $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}a_1 t^2$:

$8 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}a_1 (2)^2$

$a_1 = 4 \text{ m/s}^2$

The net force acting on the block initially is:

$F_{net} = ma_1 = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ N}$

When an additional force of $4 \text{ N}$ is applied to push the block down the incline, the new net force becomes:

$F'_{net} = F_{net} + 4 = 16 + 4 = 20 \text{ N}$

The new acceleration of the block is:

$a_2 = \dfrac{F'_{net}}{m} = \dfrac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ m/s}^2$

The distance covered by the block in the first two seconds with this new acceleration is:

$s' = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}a_2 t^2$

$s' = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}(5)(2)^2 = 10 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body slipping on a rough horizontal plane experiences a deceleration of $4.0 \text{ m/s}^2$. Determine the coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the plane.
questions.solution:
When a body slips on a rough horizontal plane, the only horizontal force acting on it is the kinetic friction.

The frictional force is given by $f_k = \mu_k N$, where $N = mg$ is the normal reaction.

The deceleration $a$ is given by Newton's second law:

$ma = \mu_k mg$

$a = \mu_k g$

Given $a = 4.0 \text{ m/s}^2$ and taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$\mu_k = \dfrac{a}{g} = \dfrac{4.0}{10} = 0.4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $m$ is positioned on a horizontal table. If the static friction coefficient is $\mu$, determine the frictional force acting on the block.
questions.solution:
The block is placed on a horizontal table and no external horizontal force is applied to it.

Frictional force comes into play only when there is relative motion or a tendency of relative motion between the surfaces in contact.

Since the applied horizontal force is zero, the tendency of relative motion is zero.

Therefore, the static frictional force acting on the block is $0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $2 \text{ kg}$ is situated on a rough inclined plane of inclination $30^\circ$. Determine the magnitude of the force parallel to the incline needed to make the block move up the incline. The coefficient of static friction is $0.2$.
questions.solution:
The force required to move the block up the rough inclined plane must overcome both the component of gravity along the incline and the maximum static friction.

The component of gravity parallel to the incline is:

$F_g = mg \sin \theta$

The normal force is:

$N = mg \cos \theta$

The maximum static friction force is:

$f_s = \mu_s N = \mu_s mg \cos \theta$

The total force $F$ required to move the block up the incline is:

$F = F_g + f_s = mg \sin \theta + \mu_s mg \cos \theta$

Substituting $m = 2 \text{ kg}$, $\theta = 30^\circ$, $\mu_s = 0.2$, and $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$F = 2 \times 9.8 \times \sin 30^\circ + 0.2 \times 2 \times 9.8 \times \cos 30^\circ$

$F = 19.6 \times 0.5 + 3.92 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

$F = 9.8 + 1.96 \times 1.732$

$F = 9.8 + 3.39 = 13.19 \text{ N}$

The closest given option is $13 \text{ N}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block is projected along a rough horizontal road at a speed of $10 \text{ m/s}$. Given that the coefficient of kinetic friction is $0.10$, how far does the block travel before coming to a stop?
questions.solution:
The initial velocity of the block is $u = 10 \text{ m/s}$.

The final velocity of the block is $v = 0 \text{ m/s}$.

The coefficient of kinetic friction is $\mu_k = 0.10$.

Taking the acceleration due to gravity as $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$, the deceleration of the block due to friction is:

$a = \mu_k g = 0.10 \times 9.8 = 0.98 \text{ m/s}^2$

Using the third equation of motion, $v^2 = u^2 - 2as$:

$0 = (10)^2 - 2(0.98)s$

$1.96s = 100$

$s = \dfrac{100}{1.96} = 51.02 \text{ m}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the distance traveled before coming to a stop is $51 \text{ m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a children's park, an inclined plane is built with an angle of incline of $45^\circ$ in its middle section, as shown in the figure. Determine the acceleration of a boy sliding on this part if the friction coefficient between the boy's clothing and the incline is $0.6$. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
The forces acting on the boy along the inclined plane are the component of gravity downwards and the frictional force upwards.

The equation of motion for the boy sliding down the incline is given by:

$ma = mg \sin \theta - f_k$

where $f_k = \mu N$ is the force of kinetic friction and $N = mg \cos \theta$ is the normal reaction.

Substituting $f_k$ into the equation, we get:

$ma = mg \sin \theta - \mu mg \cos \theta$

$a = g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta)$

Given values are $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\theta = 45^{\circ}$, and $\mu = 0.6$.

Substituting these values:

$a = 10 \left( \sin 45^{\circ} - 0.6 \cos 45^{\circ} \right)$

$a = 10 \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - 0.6 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right)$

$a = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}} (1 - 0.6)$

$a = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}} \times 0.4$

$a = \dfrac{4}{\sqrt{2}}$

$a = 2\sqrt{2} \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The angle between the resultant contact force and the normal force exerted by a body on another is called the angle of friction. If $\lambda$ is the angle of friction and $\mu$ is the coefficient of static friction, which of the following relationships is valid?
questions.solution:
Let $N$ be the normal force and $f$ be the force of friction between the two bodies.

The resultant contact force makes an angle $\lambda$ with the normal force. From the geometry of the forces, we have:

$\tan \lambda = \dfrac{f}{N}$

For static friction, the frictional force satisfies the inequality $f \leq \mu N$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of static friction.

Substituting this into the equation for $\tan \lambda$, we get:

$\tan \lambda \leq \dfrac{\mu N}{N}$

$\tan \lambda \leq \mu$

Taking the inverse tangent on both sides, we obtain:

$\lambda \leq \tan^{-1} \mu$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the arrangement depicted in the figure. Determine the tension in the string connecting the $1.0 \text{ kg}$ blocks. (Assume $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.solution:
Let the masses be $m_1 = 1.0\text{ kg}$ (left), $m_2 = 1.0\text{ kg}$ (middle), and $m_3 = 0.5\text{ kg}$ (hanging).

The maximum friction force on the two blocks on the horizontal surface is:
$f_{\text{max}} = \mu m_1 g + \mu m_2 g = 0.2 \times 1.0 \times 10 + 0.2 \times 1.0 \times 10 = 2 + 2 = 4\text{ N}$

The driving force due to the hanging block is:
$F_{\text{drive}} = m_3 g = 0.5 \times 10 = 5\text{ N}$

Since $F_{\text{drive}} > f_{\text{max}}$, the system will accelerate. Let the common acceleration be $a$.

The equation of motion for the entire system is:
$m_3 g - f_{\text{max}} = (m_1 + m_2 + m_3) a$
$5 - 4 = (1.0 + 1.0 + 0.5) a$
$1 = 2.5 a \implies a = 0.4\text{ m/s}^2$

Let $T$ be the tension in the string connecting the two $1.0\text{ kg}$ blocks. Considering the free body diagram of the left block ($m_1$):
$T - \mu m_1 g = m_1 a$
$T - 2 = 1.0 \times 0.4$
$T = 2.4\text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the arrangement depicted in the figure. Determine the tension in the string attached to the $0.50 \text{ kg}$ block. (Assume $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.solution:
The maximum frictional force acting on the two blocks on the horizontal surface is given by:

$f = \mu m_1 g + \mu m_2 g$

Substituting the given values:

$f = 0.2 \times 1.0 \times 10 + 0.2 \times 1.0 \times 10 = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ N}$

The driving force due to the hanging block is:

$W = m_3 g = 0.5 \times 10 = 5 \text{ N}$

Since the driving force is greater than the total frictional force ($5 \text{ N} > 4 \text{ N}$), the system will accelerate. Let $a$ be the common acceleration of the system. The net force on the system is:

$F_{\text{net}} = W - f = 5 - 4 = 1 \text{ N}$

The total mass of the system is:

$M = 1.0 + 1.0 + 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ kg}$

The acceleration of the system is:

$a = \dfrac{F_{\text{net}}}{M} = \dfrac{1}{2.5} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}^2$

Let $T$ be the tension in the string attached to the $0.50 \text{ kg}$ block. Considering the free body diagram of the $0.50 \text{ kg}$ block, the equation of motion is:

$m_3 g - T = m_3 a$

$5 - T = 0.5 \times 0.4$

$5 - T = 0.2$

$T = 5 - 0.2 = 4.8 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the arrangement shown in the figure, the tension in the string is $16\text{ N}$ and the acceleration of each block is $0.5 \text{ m/s}^2$. Determine the friction coefficients $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ at the two contacts with the blocks.
questions.solution:
Taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.

For the $2 \text{ kg}$ block, the equation of motion is:
$T - \mu_1 m_1 g = m_1 a$

Substituting the given values ($T = 16 \text{ N}$, $m_1 = 2 \text{ kg}$, $a = 0.5 \text{ m/s}^2$):
$16 - \mu_1 (2)(10) = 2(0.5)$
$16 - 20\mu_1 = 1$
$20\mu_1 = 15 \Rightarrow \mu_1 = 0.75$

For the $4 \text{ kg}$ block, the component of weight along the incline is $m_2 g \sin 30^\circ = 4 \times 10 \times 0.5 = 20 \text{ N}$.
Since $20 \text{ N} > 16 \text{ N}$, the block moves down the incline.

The equation of motion for the $4 \text{ kg}$ block is:
$m_2 g \sin 30^\circ - T - \mu_2 m_2 g \cos 30^\circ = m_2 a$

Substituting the given values ($m_2 = 4 \text{ kg}$, $a = 0.5 \text{ m/s}^2$):
$4(10)\sin 30^\circ - 16 - \mu_2(4)(10)\cos 30^\circ = 4(0.5)$
$20 - 16 - 40\mu_2 \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = 2$
$4 - 20\sqrt{3}\mu_2 = 2$
$20\sqrt{3}\mu_2 = 2$
$\mu_2 = \dfrac{1}{10\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.0577 \approx 0.06$

Therefore, $\mu_1 = 0.75$ and $\mu_2 = 0.06$.

Answer: $\mu_1 = 0.75$, $\mu_2 = 0.06$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The coefficient of friction between the table and the block shown in the figure is $0.2$. Calculate the tensions in the two strings.
options: $96 \text{ N}$ in the left string and $68 \text{ N}$ in the right
$68 \text{ N}$ in the left string and $96 \text{ N}$ in the right
$90 \text{ N}$ in the left string and $70 \text{ N}$ in the right
$84 \text{ N}$ in the left string and $72 \text{ N}$ in the right
questions.solution:
Let $a$ be the acceleration of the system and $T_1, T_2$ be the tensions in the left and right strings respectively. Taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.

The $15 \text{ kg}$ block moves downwards, the $5 \text{ kg}$ block on the table moves to the left, and the $5 \text{ kg}$ hanging block moves upwards.

The kinetic friction on the $5 \text{ kg}$ block on the table is:
$f = \mu N = \mu m g = 0.2 \times 5 \times 10 = 10 \text{ N}$

The equations of motion for the three blocks are:
For the $15 \text{ kg}$ block: $15g - T_1 = 15a \Rightarrow 150 - T_1 = 15a$
For the $5 \text{ kg}$ block on the table: $T_1 - T_2 - f = 5a \Rightarrow T_1 - T_2 - 10 = 5a$
For the $5 \text{ kg}$ hanging block: $T_2 - 5g = 5a \Rightarrow T_2 - 50 = 5a$

Adding the three equations:
$(150 - T_1) + (T_1 - T_2 - 10) + (T_2 - 50) = 15a + 5a + 5a$
$90 = 25a \Rightarrow a = 3.6 \text{ m/s}^2$

Substituting $a$ into the first equation:
$150 - T_1 = 15 \times 3.6 = 54 \Rightarrow T_1 = 96 \text{ N}$

Substituting $a$ into the third equation:
$T_2 - 50 = 5 \times 3.6 = 18 \Rightarrow T_2 = 68 \text{ N}$

The tension in the left string is $96 \text{ N}$ and in the right string is $68 \text{ N}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The friction coefficient between an athlete's shoes and the ground is $0.90$. Suppose a superhero wears these shoes and races for $50 \text{ m}$. There is no upper limit on his capacity for running at high speeds. Determine the minimum time he will require to complete the $50 \text{ m}$ starting from rest.
questions.solution:
The maximum acceleration of the superhero is provided by the maximum frictional force between the shoes and the ground.

The maximum frictional force is given by $f_{\text{max}} = \mu N = \mu m g$

Therefore, the maximum acceleration is $a_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{max}}}{m} = \mu g$

Substituting $\mu = 0.90$ and taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$a_{\text{max}} = 0.90 \times 10 = 9 \text{ m/s}^2$

Using the equation of kinematics for a body starting from rest ($u = 0$):

$S = u t + \dfrac{1}{2} a t^2$

$50 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2} \times 9 \times t^2$

$t^2 = \dfrac{100}{9}$

$t = \dfrac{10}{3} \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The coefficient of friction between a road and the tyre of a vehicle is $4/3$. Determine the maximum incline the road can have such that when hard brakes are applied and the wheels begin skidding, the vehicle travelling downwards at a speed of $36 \text{ km/hr}$ is stopped within $5 \text{ m}$.
questions.solution:
Initial speed of the vehicle, $u = 36 \text{ km/hr} = 36 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = 10 \text{ m/s}$

Final speed, $v = 0$

Stopping distance, $s = 5 \text{ m}$

Using the equation of motion $v^2 = u^2 - 2as$, we can find the required deceleration $a$:

$0 = (10)^2 - 2a(5)$

$10a = 100 \Rightarrow a = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$

When the vehicle is moving downwards on the incline, the component of gravity acts downwards and the kinetic friction acts upwards. The net retardation is given by:

$a = \dfrac{\mu mg \cos \theta - mg \sin \theta}{m} = g(\mu \cos \theta - \sin \theta)$

Substituting $a = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$, $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$, and $\mu = \dfrac{4}{3}$:

$10 = 10 \left( \dfrac{4}{3} \cos \theta - \sin \theta \right)$

$1 = \dfrac{4}{3} \cos \theta - \sin \theta$

$3 = 4 \cos \theta - 3 \sin \theta$

Dividing both sides by $5$:

$\dfrac{4}{5} \cos \theta - \dfrac{3}{5} \sin \theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$

$\cos 37^\circ \cos \theta - \sin 37^\circ \sin \theta = \cos 53^\circ$

$\cos(\theta + 37^\circ) = \cos 53^\circ$

$\theta + 37^\circ = 53^\circ$

$\theta = 16^\circ$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The friction coefficient between an athlete's shoes and the ground is $0.90$. Suppose a superhero wears these shoes and races for $50 \text{ m}$ starting from rest, taking exactly the minimum possible time to complete the distance. There is no upper limit on his capacity for running at high speeds. What minimum time will he take to come to a stop after completing the $50 \text{ m}$?
questions.solution:
The maximum acceleration of the superhero is provided by the friction between the shoes and the ground.

$a_{\max} = \mu g = 0.90 \times 10 = 9 \text{ m/s}^2$

To complete the $50 \text{ m}$ race in the minimum possible time, the superhero must accelerate at this maximum rate for the entire distance.

Using the equation of motion $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$, the velocity $v$ at the end of the $50 \text{ m}$ mark is:

$v^2 = 0 + 2 \times 9 \times 50 = 900$

$v = 30 \text{ m/s}$

To come to a stop in the minimum time after completing the race, the superhero must decelerate at the maximum possible rate, which is also $a_{\text{dec}} = \mu g = 9 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Using the equation $v_f = v - a_{\text{dec}}t$ for deceleration:

$0 = 30 - 9t$

$t = \dfrac{30}{9} = \dfrac{10}{3} \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Starting from rest, a block slides down an inclined surface that has an inclination of $30^\circ$ with the horizontal. It covers a distance of $8 \text{ m}$ in the first two seconds. Determine the coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the surface.
questions.solution:
Using the second equation of motion:

$s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$

Substituting the given values $s = 8 \text{ m}$, $u = 0$, and $t = 2 \text{ s}$:

$8 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}a(2)^2$

$8 = 2a \Rightarrow a = 4 \text{ m/s}^2$

The acceleration of a block sliding down a rough inclined plane is given by:

$a = g(\sin\theta - \mu_k \cos\theta)$

Substituting $a = 4 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\theta = 30^\circ$, and taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$4 = 10 \left(\dfrac{1}{2} - \mu_k \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$

$0.4 = 0.5 - \mu_k \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

$\mu_k \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 0.1$

$\mu_k = \dfrac{0.2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.11$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $2 \text{ kg}$ is placed on a rough inclined plane of inclination $30^\circ$. Find the magnitude of the horizontally applied push necessary to initiate the block's movement up the incline. The coefficient of static friction is $0.2$.
questions.solution:
Let $P$ be the magnitude of the horizontally applied push. The forces acting on the block are its weight $mg$, the normal reaction $N$, the horizontal push $P$, and the frictional force $f$.

Resolving the forces perpendicular to the inclined plane, the normal reaction is:
$N = mg \cos 30^\circ + P \sin 30^\circ$

To initiate movement up the incline, the component of the push along the incline must overcome the downward component of the weight and the limiting static friction:
$P \cos 30^\circ = mg \sin 30^\circ + f$

Since $f = \mu N$, we have:
$P \cos 30^\circ = mg \sin 30^\circ + \mu (mg \cos 30^\circ + P \sin 30^\circ)$

Rearranging to solve for $P$:
$P (\cos 30^\circ - \mu \sin 30^\circ) = mg (\sin 30^\circ + \mu \cos 30^\circ)$

$P = \dfrac{mg (\sin 30^\circ + \mu \cos 30^\circ)}{\cos 30^\circ - \mu \sin 30^\circ}$

Substituting the given values ($m = 2 \text{ kg}$, $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\mu = 0.2$):
$P = \dfrac{20 \left(0.5 + 0.2 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}{\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - 0.2 \times 0.5}$

$P = \dfrac{20 (0.5 + 0.1732)}{0.866 - 0.1}$

$P = \dfrac{20 (0.6732)}{0.766} \approx 17.57 \text{ N}$

The closest value among the given options is $17.5 \text{ N}$.

Answer: $17.5 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, two blocks are in contact sliding down an inclined surface of inclination $30^\circ$. The friction coefficient between the block of mass $2.0 \text{ kg}$ and the incline is $\mu_1 = 0.20$, and that between the block of mass $4.0 \text{ kg}$ and the incline is $\mu_2 = 0.30$. Determine the acceleration of the $2.0 \text{ kg}$ block. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let us first calculate the acceleration of each block as if they were sliding independently.

For the $2.0 \text{ kg}$ block (upper block):
$a_1 = g(\sin 30^\circ - \mu_1 \cos 30^\circ)$
$a_1 = 10 \left(0.5 - 0.20 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = 5 - \sqrt{3} \approx 3.27 \text{ m/s}^2$

For the $4.0 \text{ kg}$ block (lower block):
$a_2 = g(\sin 30^\circ - \mu_2 \cos 30^\circ)$
$a_2 = 10 \left(0.5 - 0.30 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = 5 - 1.5\sqrt{3} \approx 2.40 \text{ m/s}^2$

Since $a_1 > a_2$, the upper $2.0 \text{ kg}$ block will tend to slide faster than the lower $4.0 \text{ kg}$ block. Because it is behind the $4.0 \text{ kg}$ block, it will push against it, and both blocks will move together with a common acceleration $a$.

Considering both blocks as a single system of mass $M = m_1 + m_2 = 2.0 + 4.0 = 6.0 \text{ kg}$:
The equation of motion along the incline is:
$(m_1 + m_2)a = (m_1 + m_2)g \sin 30^\circ - f_1 - f_2$
where $f_1$ and $f_2$ are the kinetic friction forces on the two blocks.

$f_1 = \mu_1 m_1 g \cos 30^\circ = 0.20 \times 2.0 \times 10 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 2\sqrt{3} \text{ N}$
$f_2 = \mu_2 m_2 g \cos 30^\circ = 0.30 \times 4.0 \times 10 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 6\sqrt{3} \text{ N}$

Substituting these into the equation of motion:
$6.0 \times a = 6.0 \times 10 \times 0.5 - (2\sqrt{3} + 6\sqrt{3})$
$6a = 30 - 8\sqrt{3}$
$a = 5 - \dfrac{4\sqrt{3}}{3}$

Using $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$:
$a = 5 - \dfrac{4 \times 1.732}{3} = 5 - 2.309 = 2.691 \text{ m/s}^2$

Rounding to one decimal place, we get $a \approx 2.7 \text{ m/s}^2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car initiates its motion from rest on a half kilometer long bridge. The coefficient of friction between the tyre and the road is $1.0$. Determine the minimum possible time required to drive through the bridge.
questions.solution:
The maximum acceleration of the car is provided by the frictional force between the tyres and the road.

$f_{\text{max}} = \mu N = \mu mg$

$ma_{\text{max}} = \mu mg \Rightarrow a_{\text{max}} = \mu g$

Substituting the given values $\mu = 1.0$ and taking $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$a_{\text{max}} = 1.0 \times 10 = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$

The car starts from rest, so the initial velocity $u = 0$. The length of the bridge is $s = 0.5 \text{ km} = 500 \text{ m}$.

Using the equation of kinematics to find the minimum time $t$:

$s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2} a_{\text{max}} t^2$

$500 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2} (10) t^2$

$5 t^2 = 500$

$t^2 = 100$

$t = 10 \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car is travelling at a speed of $21.6 \text{ km/hr}$ when it reaches a $12.8 \text{ m}$ long slope with an inclination of $30^\circ$, as shown in the figure. The coefficient of friction between the tyre and the road is $\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}$. Determine the minimum possible speed with which the car can reach the bottom, regardless of how hard the driver applies the brakes. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Initial speed of the car, $u = 21.6 \text{ km/hr} = 21.6 \times \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s} = 6 \text{ m/s}$.

To reach the bottom with the minimum possible speed, the driver must apply the brakes as hard as possible, which means the maximum frictional force will act opposite to the direction of motion (up the slope).

The forces acting on the car along the slope are the component of gravity downwards and the frictional force upwards. The net acceleration $a$ down the slope is given by:
$a = g \sin \theta - \mu g \cos \theta$

Given $\theta = 30^\circ$, $\mu = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}$, and $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:
$a = 10 \left( \sin 30^\circ - \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}} \cos 30^\circ \right)$
$a = 10 \left( \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$
$a = 10 \left( \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{4} \right) = 10 \times \dfrac{1}{4} = 2.5 \text{ m/s}^2$

Using the third equation of motion to find the final velocity $v$ at the bottom of the slope of length $s = 12.8 \text{ m}$:
$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$
$v^2 = (6)^2 + 2(2.5)(12.8)$
$v^2 = 36 + 5 \times 12.8$
$v^2 = 36 + 64 = 100$
$v = 10 \text{ m/s}$

Converting the final speed back to km/hr:
$v = 10 \times \dfrac{18}{5} \text{ km/hr} = 36 \text{ km/hr}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two blocks are initially in contact sliding down an inclined surface of inclination $30^\circ$, where the $4.0 \text{ kg}$ block is lower on the incline than the $2.0 \text{ kg}$ block. The friction coefficient between the block of mass $2.0 \text{ kg}$ and the incline is $\mu_1 = 0.30$, and that between the block of mass $4.0 \text{ kg}$ and the incline is $\mu_2 = 0.20$. Determine the acceleration of the $2.0 \text{ kg}$ block. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let the independent accelerations of the $2.0 \text{ kg}$ and $4.0 \text{ kg}$ blocks be $a_1$ and $a_2$ respectively.

For the $2.0 \text{ kg}$ block (which is higher on the incline):
$a_1 = g \sin 30^\circ - \mu_1 g \cos 30^\circ$
$a_1 = 10 \times \dfrac{1}{2} - 0.30 \times 10 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$a_1 = 5 - 1.5 \times 1.732 = 5 - 2.598 = 2.402 \text{ m/s}^2$

For the $4.0 \text{ kg}$ block (which is lower on the incline):
$a_2 = g \sin 30^\circ - \mu_2 g \cos 30^\circ$
$a_2 = 10 \times \dfrac{1}{2} - 0.20 \times 10 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$a_2 = 5 - 1.732 = 3.268 \text{ m/s}^2$

Since $a_2 > a_1$ and the $4.0 \text{ kg}$ block is lower on the incline, the two blocks will separate and move independently.

Therefore, the acceleration of the $2.0 \text{ kg}$ block is approximately $2.4 \text{ m/s}^2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light rod connects two masses $M_1$ and $M_2$, and the system is slipping down a rough incline making an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. If the friction coefficient at both contacts is $\mu$, determine the acceleration of the system and the force exerted by the rod on one of the blocks.
questions.solution:
Let $a$ be the common acceleration of the system and $T$ be the force exerted by the rod.

The equations of motion for the two masses along the incline are:

$M_1 g \sin\theta - \mu M_1 g \cos\theta + T = M_1 a$

$M_2 g \sin\theta - \mu M_2 g \cos\theta - T = M_2 a$

Adding the two equations, we get:

$(M_1 + M_2) g \sin\theta - \mu (M_1 + M_2) g \cos\theta = (M_1 + M_2) a$

$a = g(\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)$

Substituting the value of $a$ in the first equation:

$M_1 g \sin\theta - \mu M_1 g \cos\theta + T = M_1 g(\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)$

$T = 0$

Thus, the acceleration of the system is $g(\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)$ and the force exerted by the rod is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, the friction coefficient between the board and the floor is $\mu$. Determine the maximum force the man can exert on the rope so that the board does not slip on the floor.
questions.solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the rope, which is equal to the force exerted by the man on the rope.

Let us consider the man and the board as a single system. The external forces acting on this system in the vertical direction are:
1. Total weight of the man and the board, $(M+m)g$, acting downwards.
2. Normal reaction $N$ from the floor, acting upwards.
3. Tension $T$ from the rope pulling the man upwards.

For vertical equilibrium of the system, we have:
$N + T = (M+m)g$
$\Rightarrow N = (M+m)g - T$

In the horizontal direction, the rope passes around the pulley on the floor and pulls the board to the right with a force equal to the tension $T$. To prevent slipping, the static friction $f$ between the board and the floor must act to the left and balance this pulling force.

For horizontal equilibrium, we have:
$f = T$

The condition for the board not to slip on the floor is that the required friction must be less than or equal to the maximum possible static friction:
$f \le \mu N$

Substituting the expressions for $f$ and $N$ into the inequality:
$T \le \mu [(M+m)g - T]$

Solving for $T$:
$T \le \mu(M+m)g - \mu T$
$T + \mu T \le \mu(M+m)g$
$T(1 + \mu) \le \mu(M+m)g$
$T \le \dfrac{\mu(M+m)g}{1 + \mu}$

Therefore, the maximum force the man can exert on the rope without the board slipping is $\dfrac{\mu(M+m)g}{1 + \mu}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $2 \text{ kg}$ block is positioned over a $4 \text{ kg}$ block, and both are placed on a smooth horizontal surface. The coefficient of friction between the blocks is $0.20$. Determine the acceleration of the two blocks if a horizontal force of $12 \text{ N}$ is applied to the upper block. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
options: Upper block $6 \text{ m/s}^2$, lower block $0 \text{ m/s}^2$
Upper block $4 \text{ m/s}^2$, lower block $1 \text{ m/s}^2$
Upper block $2 \text{ m/s}^2$, lower block $2 \text{ m/s}^2$
Upper block $1 \text{ m/s}^2$, lower block $4 \text{ m/s}^2$
questions.solution:
The maximum friction force between the two blocks is given by $f_{\text{max}} = \mu m_1 g$

$f_{\text{max}} = 0.20 \times 2 \times 10 = 4 \text{ N}$

Assuming both blocks move together, their common acceleration would be $a = \dfrac{F}{m_1 + m_2}$

$a = \dfrac{12}{2 + 4} = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$

The force required to move the lower block with this acceleration is $f = m_2 a = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ N}$

Since $f > f_{\text{max}}$, the blocks will slip over each other. The friction force acting between them will be $f_k = 4 \text{ N}$.

For the upper block, the equation of motion is $F - f_k = m_1 a_1$

$12 - 4 = 2 a_1 \Rightarrow a_1 = 4 \text{ m/s}^2$

For the lower block, the equation of motion is $f_k = m_2 a_2$

$4 = 4 a_2 \Rightarrow a_2 = 1 \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $2 \text{ kg}$ block is positioned over a $4 \text{ kg}$ block, and both are placed on a smooth horizontal surface. The coefficient of friction between the blocks is $0.20$. Determine the acceleration of the two blocks if a horizontal force of $12 \text{ N}$ is applied to the lower block. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
options: Upper block $0 \text{ m/s}^2$, lower block $3 \text{ m/s}^2$
Upper block $1 \text{ m/s}^2$, lower block $2.5 \text{ m/s}^2$
Upper block $2 \text{ m/s}^2$, lower block $2 \text{ m/s}^2$
Upper block $4 \text{ m/s}^2$, lower block $1 \text{ m/s}^2$
questions.solution:
Let $m_1 = 2 \text{ kg}$ be the mass of the upper block and $m_2 = 4 \text{ kg}$ be the mass of the lower block.

The maximum static friction between the two blocks is given by $f_{\text{max}} = \mu m_1 g = 0.20 \times 2 \times 10 = 4 \text{ N}$.

Assuming both blocks move together as a single system, their common acceleration $a$ is:

$a = \dfrac{F}{m_1 + m_2} = \dfrac{12}{2 + 4} = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$

The frictional force required to provide this acceleration to the upper block is:

$f_{\text{req}} = m_1 a = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ N}$

Since the required friction $f_{\text{req}}$ is equal to the maximum available static friction $f_{\text{max}}$, the blocks will not slip relative to each other.

Therefore, both the upper and lower blocks will move with an acceleration of $2 \text{ m/s}^2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the arrangement depicted in the figure. Determine the acceleration of the $1.0 \text{ kg}$ blocks. (Assume $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.solution:
Let the masses of the blocks on the horizontal surface be $m_1 = 1.0 \text{ kg}$ and $m_2 = 1.0 \text{ kg}$.

Let the mass of the hanging block be $m_3 = 0.5 \text{ kg}$.

The driving force for the system is the weight of the hanging block:

$F_{\text{driving}} = m_3 g = 0.5 \times 10 = 5 \text{ N}$

The frictional force opposing the motion acts on both blocks on the horizontal surface. The total frictional force is:

$f_k = \mu m_1 g + \mu m_2 g = \mu (m_1 + m_2) g$

$f_k = 0.2 \times (1.0 + 1.0) \times 10 = 4 \text{ N}$

Since the driving force ($5 \text{ N}$) is greater than the total frictional force ($4 \text{ N}$), the system will accelerate.

The net force acting on the system is:

$F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{driving}} - f_k = 5 - 4 = 1 \text{ N}$

The total mass of the system moving together is:

$M_{\text{total}} = m_1 + m_2 + m_3 = 1.0 + 1.0 + 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ kg}$

The acceleration $a$ of the system is given by Newton's second law:

$a = \dfrac{F_{\text{net}}}{M_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{1}{2.5} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three blocks of masses $2 \text{ kg}$, $3 \text{ kg}$, and $7 \text{ kg}$ are stacked one over the other from top to bottom on a horizontal surface. The coefficient of friction between the $2 \text{ kg}$ and $3 \text{ kg}$ blocks is $0.2$, between the $3 \text{ kg}$ and $7 \text{ kg}$ blocks is $0.3$, and between the $7 \text{ kg}$ block and the horizontal surface is $0.0$. Determine the respective accelerations $a_1$, $a_2$, and $a_3$ of the $2 \text{ kg}$, $3 \text{ kg}$, and $7 \text{ kg}$ blocks when a horizontal force of $10 \text{ N}$ is applied to the $3 \text{ kg}$ block. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let the masses of the blocks be $m_1 = 2 \text{ kg}$, $m_2 = 3 \text{ kg}$, and $m_3 = 7 \text{ kg}$.

The maximum static friction force between the $2 \text{ kg}$ and $3 \text{ kg}$ blocks is:

$f_{1,\max} = \mu_1 m_1 g = 0.2 \times 2 \times 10 = 4 \text{ N}$

The maximum static friction force between the $3 \text{ kg}$ and $7 \text{ kg}$ blocks is:

$f_{2,\max} = \mu_2 (m_1 + m_2) g = 0.3 \times (2 + 3) \times 10 = 15 \text{ N}$

Assume all three blocks move together as a single system. The common acceleration $a$ is given by the total applied force divided by the total mass:

$a = \dfrac{F}{m_1 + m_2 + m_3} = \dfrac{10}{2 + 3 + 7} = \dfrac{10}{12} = \dfrac{5}{6} \text{ m/s}^2$

Now, check the required friction forces to keep the blocks moving together with this acceleration.

For the $2 \text{ kg}$ block, the required friction force $f_1$ to accelerate it at $\dfrac{5}{6} \text{ m/s}^2$ is:

$f_1 = m_1 a = 2 \times \dfrac{5}{6} = \dfrac{5}{3} \text{ N}$

Since $f_1 < f_{1,\max}$ ($\dfrac{5}{3} \text{ N} < 4 \text{ N}$), the $2 \text{ kg}$ block does not slip on the $3 \text{ kg}$ block.

For the $7 \text{ kg}$ block, the required friction force $f_2$ to accelerate it at $\dfrac{5}{6} \text{ m/s}^2$ is:

$f_2 = m_3 a = 7 \times \dfrac{5}{6} = \dfrac{35}{6} \text{ N}$

Since $f_2 < f_{2,\max}$ ($\dfrac{35}{6} \text{ N} < 15 \text{ N}$), the $7 \text{ kg}$ block does not slip on the $3 \text{ kg}$ block.

Therefore, the assumption is correct, and all three blocks move together with the same acceleration:

$a_1 = a_2 = a_3 = \dfrac{5}{6} \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the accelerations $a_1$, $a_2$, and $a_3$ of the three blocks illustrated in the figure when a horizontal force of $10 \text{ N}$ is applied to the $2 \text{ kg}$ block. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
First, we find the maximum possible friction forces at each interface.

Between the $2 \text{ kg}$ and $3 \text{ kg}$ blocks:
$f_{1,\text{max}} = \mu_1 m_1 g = 0.2 \times 2 \times 10 = 4 \text{ N}$

Between the $3 \text{ kg}$ and $7 \text{ kg}$ blocks:
$f_{2,\text{max}} = \mu_2 (m_1 + m_2) g = 0.3 \times (2 + 3) \times 10 = 15 \text{ N}$

Between the $7 \text{ kg}$ block and the ground:
$f_{3,\text{max}} = \mu_3 (m_1 + m_2 + m_3) g = 0 \times 12 \times 10 = 0 \text{ N}$

Let us assume all three blocks move together with a common acceleration $a$.
$a = \dfrac{F}{m_1 + m_2 + m_3} = \dfrac{10}{2 + 3 + 7} = \dfrac{10}{12} = \dfrac{5}{6} \text{ m/s}^2$

The required friction force $f_1$ to keep the $2 \text{ kg}$ block moving with this acceleration is:
$F - f_1 = m_1 a \Rightarrow 10 - f_1 = 2 \times \dfrac{5}{6} \Rightarrow f_1 = 10 - \dfrac{5}{3} = \dfrac{25}{3} \approx 8.33 \text{ N}$

Since $f_1 > f_{1,\text{max}}$ ($8.33 \text{ N} > 4 \text{ N}$), the $2 \text{ kg}$ block will slip relative to the $3 \text{ kg}$ block. Thus, kinetic friction $f_1 = 4 \text{ N}$ acts between them.

The acceleration of the $2 \text{ kg}$ block is:
$a_1 = \dfrac{F - f_1}{m_1} = \dfrac{10 - 4}{2} = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$

Now, the friction force $f_1 = 4 \text{ N}$ acts in the forward direction on the $3 \text{ kg}$ block. We check if the $3 \text{ kg}$ and $7 \text{ kg}$ blocks move together.
The maximum friction available between them is $f_{2,\text{max}} = 15 \text{ N}$. Since the driving force on the $3 \text{ kg}$ block is only $4 \text{ N}$, which is less than $15 \text{ N}$, they will not slip relative to each other and will move together.

The common acceleration of the $3 \text{ kg}$ and $7 \text{ kg}$ blocks is:
$a_2 = a_3 = \dfrac{f_1}{m_2 + m_3} = \dfrac{4}{3 + 7} = \dfrac{4}{10} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}^2$

(To verify, the friction force $f_2$ required between them is $m_3 a_3 = 7 \times 0.4 = 2.8 \text{ N}$, which is well below the maximum limit of $15 \text{ N}$).

Therefore, $a_1 = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$, $a_2 = 0.4 \text{ m/s}^2$, and $a_3 = 0.4 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Answer: $a_1 = 3 \text{ m/s}^2$, $a_2 = 0.4 \text{ m/s}^2$, $a_3 = 0.4 \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body begins slipping down an incline, travelling half a meter in half a second. How much time will it require to move the subsequent half meter?
questions.solution:
Since the body starts from rest, the distance covered is given by $s = \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$, which implies $t \propto \sqrt{s}$.

Let $t_1$ be the time taken to cover the first distance $s_1 = 0.5$ m, and $t_2$ be the total time taken to cover the total distance $s_2 = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1$ m.

$\dfrac{t_2}{t_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{s_2}{s_1}}$

$\dfrac{t_2}{0.5} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{0.5}} = \sqrt{2}$

$t_2 = 0.5\sqrt{2} \approx 0.5 \times 1.414 = 0.707$ s

The time required to move the subsequent half meter is:

$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = 0.707 - 0.5 = 0.207$ s

Rounding off to two decimal places, we get $0.21$ s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three blocks of masses $2 \text{ kg}$, $3 \text{ kg}$, and $7 \text{ kg}$ are stacked one over the other from top to bottom on a horizontal surface. The coefficient of friction between the $2 \text{ kg}$ and $3 \text{ kg}$ blocks is $0.2$, between the $3 \text{ kg}$ and $7 \text{ kg}$ blocks is $0.3$, and between the $7 \text{ kg}$ block and the horizontal surface is $0.0$. Determine the respective accelerations $a_1$, $a_2$, and $a_3$ of the $2 \text{ kg}$, $3 \text{ kg}$, and $7 \text{ kg}$ blocks when a horizontal force of $10 \text{ N}$ is applied to the $7 \text{ kg}$ block. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let the masses of the blocks be $m_1 = 2 \text{ kg}$, $m_2 = 3 \text{ kg}$, and $m_3 = 7 \text{ kg}$.

First, we calculate the maximum possible static friction forces between the surfaces.

The normal force between the $2 \text{ kg}$ and $3 \text{ kg}$ blocks is $N_1 = m_1 g = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ N}$.

Maximum friction between them: $f_{1,\text{max}} = \mu_1 N_1 = 0.2 \times 20 = 4 \text{ N}$.

The normal force between the $3 \text{ kg}$ and $7 \text{ kg}$ blocks is $N_2 = (m_1 + m_2) g = (2 + 3) \times 10 = 50 \text{ N}$.

Maximum friction between them: $f_{2,\text{max}} = \mu_2 N_2 = 0.3 \times 50 = 15 \text{ N}$.

Let us assume that all three blocks move together as a single system under the applied force $F = 10 \text{ N}$.

The common acceleration would be:

$a = \dfrac{F}{m_1 + m_2 + m_3} = \dfrac{10}{2 + 3 + 7} = \dfrac{10}{12} = \dfrac{5}{6} \text{ m/s}^2$

Now, we verify if the required friction forces to maintain this common acceleration are within their maximum limits.

For the $2 \text{ kg}$ block to accelerate at $a = \dfrac{5}{6} \text{ m/s}^2$, the required friction force is:

$f_1 = m_1 a = 2 \times \dfrac{5}{6} = \dfrac{5}{3} \text{ N}$

Since $f_1 \le f_{1,\text{max}}$ ($\dfrac{5}{3} \text{ N} \le 4 \text{ N}$), the $2 \text{ kg}$ block does not slip on the $3 \text{ kg}$ block.

For the combined system of $2 \text{ kg}$ and $3 \text{ kg}$ blocks to accelerate at $a = \dfrac{5}{6} \text{ m/s}^2$, the required friction force from the $7 \text{ kg}$ block is:

$f_2 = (m_1 + m_2) a = (2 + 3) \times \dfrac{5}{6} = \dfrac{25}{6} \text{ N}$

Since $f_2 \le f_{2,\text{max}}$ ($\dfrac{25}{6} \text{ N} \le 15 \text{ N}$), the $3 \text{ kg}$ block does not slip on the $7 \text{ kg}$ block.

Therefore, our assumption is correct, and all three blocks move together with the same acceleration.

$a_1 = a_2 = a_3 = \dfrac{5}{6} \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The coefficient of friction between the two blocks displayed in the figure is $\mu$, whereas the floor is smooth. Assume the complete system is situated inside an elevator that is descending with an acceleration $a < g$. Determine the maximum horizontal force $F$ that can be applied without disrupting the equilibrium of the system. Subsequently, if the applied horizontal force is twice this maximum value, find the magnitude of the accelerations of the two masses.
options: $\mu m(g - a)$ and $\dfrac{\mu m(g - a)}{m + M}$
$2\mu m(g - a)$ and $\dfrac{2\mu m(g - a)}{m + M}$
$2\mu m(g + a)$ and $\dfrac{2\mu m(g + a)}{m + M}$
questions.solution:
In the frame of the descending elevator, the effective acceleration due to gravity is $g_{eff} = g - a$.

The normal reaction between the block of mass $m$ and the block of mass $M$ is $N = m(g - a)$.

The maximum static friction force between the two blocks is $f_{max} = \mu N = \mu m(g - a)$.

Let $T$ be the tension in the string. For the system to remain in equilibrium, the net force on each block must be zero. With the impending motion of $m$ to the left, the friction force $f$ on $m$ acts to the right, and the friction force on $M$ acts to the left.

For block $M$ in the horizontal direction:
$T - f = 0 \Rightarrow T = f$

For block $m$ in the horizontal direction:
$F - T - f = 0 \Rightarrow F = 2f$

The maximum horizontal force $F_{max}$ without disrupting equilibrium corresponds to $f = f_{max}$:
$F_{max} = 2f_{max} = 2\mu m(g - a)$

When the applied force is twice this maximum value, $F = 2F_{max} = 4\mu m(g - a)$. The system accelerates. Let $a'$ be the magnitude of the acceleration of the masses. Block $m$ accelerates to the left and block $M$ accelerates to the right with the same magnitude $a'$.

The equations of motion for the blocks are:
For block $M$: $T - f_{max} = M a'$
For block $m$: $F - T - f_{max} = m a'$

Adding these two equations yields:
$F - 2f_{max} = (m + M)a'$

Substituting the values of $F$ and $f_{max}$:
$4\mu m(g - a) - 2\mu m(g - a) = (m + M)a'$

$2\mu m(g - a) = (m + M)a'$

$a' = \dfrac{2\mu m(g - a)}{m + M}$

Answer: $2\mu m(g - a)$ and $\dfrac{2\mu m(g - a)}{m + M}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the acceleration of the block of mass $M$ in the arrangement depicted in the figure. The coefficient of friction between the two blocks is $\mu_1$, while the coefficient of friction between the larger block and the ground is $\mu_2$.
questions.solution:
Let $a$ be the acceleration of block $M$ towards the right.

Let $x$ be the distance of block $M$ from the wall and $y$ be the distance of block $m$ below the pulley on $M$. The total length of the string is $L = 2x + y + \text{constant}$.

Differentiating twice with respect to time, we get $0 = -2a + a_{y,\text{rel}}$, which implies the downward acceleration of $m$ relative to $M$ is $a_{y,\text{rel}} = 2a$.

The absolute horizontal acceleration of $m$ is $a$ (towards the right), and its absolute vertical acceleration is $2a$ (downwards).

For block $m$ in the horizontal direction, the only force is the normal reaction $N$ from $M$:
$N = ma$

For block $m$ in the vertical direction, the forces are gravity $mg$ (downwards), tension $T$ (upwards), and friction $f_1$ (upwards):
$mg - T - f_1 = m(2a)$

Since $f_1 = \mu_1 N = \mu_1 ma$, we have:
$T = mg - 2ma - \mu_1 ma = m[g - a(2 + \mu_1)]$

For the system of blocks $(M+m)$ in the vertical direction, the internal forces (tension $T$ and friction $f_1$ between $M$ and $m$) cancel out. The net vertical acceleration of the center of mass is due to the downward acceleration of $m$.
The normal reaction from the ground is $N_{\text{ground}}$.
$(M+m)g - N_{\text{ground}} = m(2a) + M(0)$
$N_{\text{ground}} = (M+m)g - 2ma$

The friction force from the ground on $M$ is:
$f_2 = \mu_2 N_{\text{ground}} = \mu_2 [(M+m)g - 2ma]$

For the system $(M+m)$ in the horizontal direction, the external forces are the tensions from the two horizontal string segments pulling to the right, and the ground friction $f_2$ pulling to the left:
$2T - f_2 = (M+m)a$

Substituting the expressions for $T$ and $f_2$ into the horizontal equation:
$2m[g - a(2 + \mu_1)] - \mu_2 [(M+m)g - 2ma] = (M+m)a$

Expanding and rearranging the terms:
$2mg - 4ma - 2\mu_1 ma - \mu_2(M+m)g + 2\mu_2 ma = Ma + ma$

$2mg - \mu_2(M+m)g = Ma + 5ma + 2\mu_1 ma - 2\mu_2 ma$

$g[2m - \mu_2(M+m)] = a[M + m(5 + 2\mu_1 - 2\mu_2)]$

$a = \dfrac{[2m - \mu_2(M+m)]g}{M + m[5 + 2(\mu_1 - \mu_2)]}$

Answer: $\dfrac{[2m - \mu_2(M+m)]g}{M + m[5 + 2(\mu_1 - \mu_2)]}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person ($40 \text{ kg}$) manages to remain at rest between two vertical walls by pressing one wall $A$ with his hands and feet and the other wall $B$ with his back. The friction coefficient between his body and the walls is $0.8$, and limiting friction acts at all contact points. Determine the normal force exerted by either wall on the person. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let the normal force exerted by wall $A$ on the person be $N_A$ and by wall $B$ be $N_B$.

For horizontal equilibrium, the net horizontal force on the person must be zero. Therefore, the normal forces exerted by the two walls must be equal:
$N_A = N_B = N$

For vertical equilibrium, the total upward frictional force must balance the downward weight of the person. The weight of the person is $W = mg = 40 \times 10 = 400 \text{ N}$.

Since limiting friction acts at all contact points, the frictional forces at walls $A$ and $B$ are:
$f_A = \mu N_A = \mu N$
$f_B = \mu N_B = \mu N$

Equating the total upward frictional force to the weight:
$f_A + f_B = mg$
$\mu N + \mu N = mg$
$2\mu N = mg$

Substituting the given values ($\mu = 0.8$, $mg = 400 \text{ N}$):
$2 \times 0.8 \times N = 400$
$1.6 N = 400$
$N = \dfrac{400}{1.6} = 250 \text{ N}$

Thus, the normal force exerted by either wall on the person is $250 \text{ N}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $2 \text{ kg}$ block is pressed against a rough vertical wall by a perpendicular force of $40 \text{ N}$. The coefficient of static friction is $0.5$. A second horizontal force of $15 \text{ N}$ is applied to the block, acting parallel to the wall. Determine if the block will move. If it moves, find its direction relative to the $15 \text{ N}$ force; if it does not move, find the magnitude of the frictional force exerted by the wall. (Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$)
options: It moves at an angle of $53^\circ$ with the $15 \text{ N}$ force.
It remains stationary, and the frictional force is $20 \text{ N}$.
It moves at an angle of $37^\circ$ with the $15 \text{ N}$ force.
It remains stationary, and the frictional force is $25 \text{ N}$.
questions.solution:
Normal force exerted by the wall on the block is $N = 40 \text{ N}$.

Maximum static frictional force is $f_{max} = \mu_s N = 0.5 \times 40 = 20 \text{ N}$.

The forces acting on the block parallel to the wall are its weight $W = mg = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ N}$ acting vertically downwards and the applied horizontal force $F = 15 \text{ N}$.

The net driving force on the block is $F_{net} = \sqrt{F^2 + W^2} = \sqrt{15^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{225 + 400} = 25 \text{ N}$.

Since $F_{net} > f_{max}$, the block will move.

The direction of motion will be along the net driving force. Let $\theta$ be the angle it makes with the $15 \text{ N}$ force.

$\tan \theta = \dfrac{W}{F} = \dfrac{20}{15} = \dfrac{4}{3}$

$\theta = 53^\circ$

Thus, the block moves at an angle of $53^\circ$ with the $15 \text{ N}$ force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person ($40 \text{ kg}$) manages to remain at rest between two vertical walls by pressing one wall $A$ with his hands and feet and the other wall $B$ with his back, as shown in the figure. The friction coefficient between his body and the walls is $0.8$, and limiting friction acts at all contact points. Let $F_A$ and $F_B$ represent the forces with which the person pushes wall $A$ and wall $B$, respectively. Which of the following relationships is correct?
questions.solution:
For the person to remain at rest, the net force acting on him in any direction must be zero.

Considering the horizontal direction, the only forces acting on the person are the normal reactions from the vertical walls:
1. Normal reaction $N_A$ from wall A acting towards the left.
2. Normal reaction $N_B$ from wall B acting towards the right.

For horizontal equilibrium, we must have:
$\sum F_x = 0$
$N_B - N_A = 0$
$N_A = N_B$

By Newton's third law, the normal force the person exerts on wall A is $F_A = N_A$, and the normal force he exerts on wall B is $F_B = N_B$.

Therefore, it directly follows that:
$F_A = F_B$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small block of mass $m$ is placed at the left end of a larger block of mass $M$ and length $l$, as shown in the figure. The system is capable of sliding on a horizontal road. It is given an initial velocity $v$ towards the right. The coefficient of friction between the bigger block and the road is $\mu$, while the coefficient of friction between the two blocks is $\mu/2$. Determine the time elapsed before the smaller block separates from the larger block.
questions.solution:
Let the right direction be positive. Both blocks initially move to the right with velocity $v$.

The frictional force exerted by the road on the larger block $M$ acts towards the left:
$f_1 = \mu(M+m)g$

If both blocks were to move together, their common acceleration would be $-\mu g$. The force required to keep the smaller block $m$ moving with this acceleration is $-\mu mg$. However, the maximum static friction available between the blocks is $\dfrac{\mu}{2}mg$. Since the required force exceeds the available friction, the blocks will slip relative to each other.

The smaller block $m$ experiences kinetic friction from $M$ acting towards the left:
$f_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{2}mg$

By Newton's third law, the smaller block $m$ exerts an equal and opposite frictional force $f_2$ on $M$ towards the right.

The acceleration of the smaller block $m$ is:
$a_m = \dfrac{-f_2}{m} = -\dfrac{\mu g}{2}$

The net force on the larger block $M$ is the sum of the friction from the road and the friction from $m$:
$F_M = f_2 - f_1 = \dfrac{\mu}{2}mg - \mu(M+m)g$

The acceleration of the larger block $M$ is:
$a_M = \dfrac{F_M}{M} = \dfrac{\dfrac{\mu}{2}mg - \mu Mg - \mu mg}{M} = -\mu g - \dfrac{\mu mg}{2M}$

The relative acceleration of $m$ with respect to $M$ is:
$a_{rel} = a_m - a_M = -\dfrac{\mu g}{2} - \left(-\mu g - \dfrac{\mu mg}{2M}\right)$

$a_{rel} = \dfrac{\mu g}{2} + \dfrac{\mu mg}{2M} = \dfrac{\mu g(M+m)}{2M}$

The initial relative velocity is $u_{rel} = v - v = 0$. The relative distance the smaller block must cover to separate from the larger block is $s_{rel} = l$.

Using the kinematic equation $s_{rel} = u_{rel}t + \dfrac{1}{2}a_{rel}t^2$:
$l = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \dfrac{\mu g(M+m)}{2M} \right] t^2$

$t^2 = \dfrac{4Ml}{\mu g(M+m)}$

$t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Ml}{\mu g(M+m)}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $M$ is placed on a rough horizontal surface. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the surface is $\mu$. The block is to be pulled by applying a force to it. Determine the minimum force required to slide the block, and the direction in which this force should act.
options: $\mu Mg$ at an angle of $0^\circ$ with the horizontal
$\dfrac{\mu Mg}{\sqrt{1 - \mu^2}}$ at an angle $\sin^{-1}\mu$ with the horizontal
$\dfrac{\mu Mg}{\sqrt{1 + \mu^2}}$ at an angle $\tan^{-1}\mu$ with the horizontal
$\dfrac{Mg}{\sqrt{1 + \mu^2}}$ at an angle $\cot^{-1}\mu$ with the horizontal
questions.solution:
Let the force $F$ be applied at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.

The normal reaction $N$ from the surface is given by equating vertical forces:

$N + F \sin\theta = Mg \Rightarrow N = Mg - F \sin\theta$

The maximum static frictional force is:

$f = \mu N = \mu (Mg - F \sin\theta)$

For the block to just slide, the horizontal component of the applied force must be equal to the maximum static friction:

$F \cos\theta = \mu (Mg - F \sin\theta)$

$F \cos\theta + \mu F \sin\theta = \mu Mg$

$F (\cos\theta + \mu \sin\theta) = \mu Mg$

$F = \dfrac{\mu Mg}{\cos\theta + \mu \sin\theta}$

To find the minimum force, the denominator $\cos\theta + \mu \sin\theta$ must be maximum. The maximum value of $a \cos\theta + b \sin\theta$ is $\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$.

Maximum value of $\cos\theta + \mu \sin\theta = \sqrt{1 + \mu^2}$

This maximum occurs when $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\mu}{1} = \mu \Rightarrow \theta = \tan^{-1}\mu$

Substituting the maximum value of the denominator, the minimum force is:

$F_{min} = \dfrac{\mu Mg}{\sqrt{1 + \mu^2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The friction coefficient between the two blocks shown in the figure is $\mu$, while the floor is smooth. Determine the maximum horizontal force $F$ that can be applied without disturbing the equilibrium of the system. Additionally, if the applied horizontal force is double this maximum value, find the magnitude of the accelerations of the two masses.
options: Maximum force = $\mu mg$, Acceleration = $\dfrac{\mu mg}{M + m}$
Maximum force = $2\mu mg$, Acceleration = $\dfrac{\mu mg}{M + m}$
Maximum force = $2\mu mg$, Acceleration = $\dfrac{2\mu mg}{M + m}$
Maximum force = $\mu mg$, Acceleration = $\dfrac{2\mu mg}{M + m}$
questions.solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string and $f$ be the frictional force between the two blocks.

For the system to remain in equilibrium, both blocks must be at rest.

Considering the forces on the upper block $m$ in the horizontal direction, the applied force $F$ acts to the left, while the tension $T$ and static friction $f$ act to the right.

$F = T + f$

Considering the forces on the lower block $M$ in the horizontal direction, the tension $T$ acts to the right, and the static friction $f$ exerted by $m$ acts to the left.

$T = f$

Substituting $T = f$ into the first equation, we get:

$F = 2f$

The maximum value of static friction is $f = \mu mg$.

Therefore, the maximum horizontal force $F$ that can be applied without disturbing equilibrium is:

$F_{\text{max}} = 2\mu mg$

When the applied force is doubled, the new force is $F' = 4\mu mg$. The blocks will now accelerate. Let $a$ be the acceleration of block $m$ to the left. Due to the string constraint, block $M$ will accelerate to the right with the same magnitude $a$.

The kinetic friction between the blocks is $f = \mu mg$.

Writing the equations of motion for both blocks:

For block $m$ moving to the left:

$F' - T - f = ma$

$4\mu mg - T - \mu mg = ma$

$3\mu mg - T = ma$

For block $M$ moving to the right:

$T - f = Ma$

$T - \mu mg = Ma$

Adding the two equations of motion:

$(3\mu mg - T) + (T - \mu mg) = ma + Ma$

$2\mu mg = (M + m)a$

Solving for the acceleration $a$:

$a = \dfrac{2\mu mg}{M + m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Consider the arrangement illustrated in the figure. Suppose a small electric field $E$ acts in the vertically upward direction, and the upper block has a positive charge $Q$ on its top surface. The friction coefficient between the two blocks is $\mu$, while the floor is smooth. Determine the maximum horizontal force $F$ that can be applied without disturbing the equilibrium.
[Hint: The force exerted on a charge $Q$ by the electric field $E$ is $F = QE$ in the direction of $E$.]
questions.solution:
Let $N$ be the normal reaction between the upper block of mass $m$ and the lower block of mass $M$.

For the upper block, the forces in the vertical direction are its weight $mg$ acting downwards, the normal reaction $N$ acting upwards, and the electric force $F_e = QE$ acting upwards (since the charge is positive and the electric field is vertically upward).
For vertical equilibrium of the upper block:
$N + QE = mg$
$\Rightarrow N = mg - QE$

The maximum static friction force between the two blocks is:
$f_{\max} = \mu N = \mu(mg - QE)$

Let $T$ be the tension in the string. When the maximum horizontal force $F$ is applied to the left on the upper block, it tends to slide to the left relative to the lower block. Thus, the friction force $f$ on the upper block acts to the right.
For the horizontal equilibrium of the upper block:
$F = T + f$

For the lower block, the string pulls it to the right with tension $T$, and the friction force $f$ exerted by the upper block acts to the left. Since the floor is smooth, there is no friction from the floor.
For the horizontal equilibrium of the lower block:
$T = f$

Substituting $T = f$ into the equation for the upper block, we get:
$F = f + f = 2f$

To find the maximum force $F$ without disturbing the equilibrium, the friction must be at its limiting value, $f = f_{\max}$.
$F_{\max} = 2f_{\max} = 2\mu(mg - QE)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Under the influence of an applied horizontal force, a block of mass $m$ slips on a rough horizontal table. The coefficient of friction between the block and the table is $\mu$. Assuming the table remains stationary on the floor, determine the total frictional force exerted by the floor on the legs of the table.
questions.solution:
As the block of mass $m$ slips on the rough horizontal table, the kinetic frictional force exerted by the table on the block is $f = \mu mg$.

According to Newton's third law of motion, the block exerts an equal and opposite frictional force of magnitude $f = \mu mg$ on the table in the horizontal direction.

Since the table remains stationary on the floor, its horizontal acceleration is zero, meaning the net horizontal force acting on the table must be zero.

The only horizontal forces acting on the table are the friction from the block and the static friction from the floor. Therefore, the total frictional force exerted by the floor on the legs of the table must perfectly balance the frictional force exerted by the block.

Thus, the magnitude of the frictional force exerted by the floor is $\mu mg$.
